Mark's ending hammers home a hard truth: the Resurrection doesn't automatically produce faith. The women encounter an angel at an empty tomb and hear the words every promise hinged on—"He has risen!"—yet they flee in terror, saying nothing to anyone. When Jesus Himself appears to Mary Magdalene, then to two disciples on the road, those who see Him are not believed. The other disciples, locked in grief and doubt, reject the testimony of eyewitnesses. Even after everything—after the crucifixion, the empty tomb, the angelic announcement, multiple appearances—the disciples don't automatically surrender to faith. Jesus has to rebuke them for their unbelief and hardness of heart. The Resurrection has happened. The evidence is there. Yet belief is not automatic. Something deeper is required of these men than mere empirical observation.

This is where the Commission comes in, and it cuts both ways. Jesus commands His disciples to go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature. But He also makes clear that not everyone will believe. "Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned." There is no middle ground here, no comfortable neutrality. The message is absolute. The world will be divided by the gospel into those who believe and are saved, and those who reject it and face judgment. The disciples are being sent out not to convince everyone, but to preach the truth and let that truth do its work—saving some and condemning others.

The signs Jesus describes—driving out demons, speaking in new tongues, protection from harm, healing the sick—accompany those who believe. These are not guarantees of ease or prosperity, but markers of God's power confirming His Word. Yet Mark's ending doesn't linger on the miraculous. It moves quickly to the Ascension: the Lord Jesus sat down at the right hand of God. The disciples go out, they preach everywhere, and the Lord works through them. The narrative ends not with triumph or resolution, but with a simple statement of obedience and God's faithfulness. The disciples had doubted. They had been rebuked. But they went anyway. And God confirmed His Word. That is where faith leads—not to the comfort of certainty, but to the costly obedience of proclaiming a risen Christ to a world bent on disbelief.
